speaker: interviewer
text: January 14th, 1996. Dorothy Grinims--

speaker: interviewee
text: I go from the beginning?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is being interviewed by Abby Allen. OK. So you wanted to tell me about the first execution.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Some friends went to Ostrog a few years ago. And they spoke to some-- to people that were witnessing the executions. And they said that a day or two later, they saw the earth was moving as evidence that some of them were still alive in the graves. That's the grave that my father was.

speaker: interviewer
text: That they had buried him alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not, they were not completely dead when they were buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go back to visit that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never wanted to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I did have recently a memorial. Some friends in Israel organized a memorial. And they all went back for the memorial, which I'm going to get tapes pretty soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we're back. We're a year later, right? This is 1942 when there-- when it's the third execution.

speaker: interviewee
text: Third execution, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you were telling me a little bit during the break about the ghetto again. You said--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now it's a break?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no. Before. This is-- we're on tape. During our break before, you were telling me about how unsanitary the ghetto was and the conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had an outhouse. We didn't have toilet facilities. And then there was no hot water, and no heat, and very bad-- terrible, terrible sanitary conditions. But somehow, we survived. And then started a third execution. And then my, my husband-- at that time, he was my boyfriend-- he came in. And he says, let's go. And said, I cannot leave my mother. And I asked my mother to go with us to escape from the ghetto. And my mother refused to go. And she said, I'm getting too old. I don't have the strength. I'll never survive. You are young. You will go with Mark, and try to survive, and try to escape, and try to be alive to tell the family in, in America and tell the world how the Nazis tortured us and what a-- how they persecuted us. And then I kissed her goodbye. And I left. And I ran away with my husband. And the ghetto was surrounded by Ukrainian policemen. And he bribed them, one or two, with gold coins. And he let us escape. And we escaped to the house of this Mrs. Stepaniuk, this policeman's mother. He wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't-- before the third execution began, you didn't see any of it? You mean, you just knew it was coming, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We just-- we were told. We knew it was coming, because they were chasing people out of their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left while all this was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I-- while all this, we left. It was a terrible chaos. Everybody was running and screaming. We didn't know what's going on. And then we got to the gate with the barbed wire. And my husband gave him the gold coins. And he let us get out of the ghetto. And then we ran into-- through fields. We ran to the house where this Stepaniuk lived. She let us in. And she put us in a basement. And she gave us food. And we spent the whole day until the evening in the basement. And I heard the shots in the ghetto. And lights-- there were lights. And we saw lights. And we, and we heard shots. And at that point, I knew that my mother was being executed. And we stayed there till the evening. She gave us food. And she said, I can't keep you. Because somebody saw you come in here. And they're going to come and get you. You have to get away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she know that someone saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because a neighbor told her. A neighbor told her that they saw us. Because we were jumping from, from-- what do you call that-- from gates, to gates, from the people's gardens, you know? They saw us escaping there. And she told us we have to leave. And then we left. And then we went to another Ukrainian friend of my husband who put us in the cellar. He had other Jewish people he was hiding. And, and that's where we spent time in the basement, in the cellar, on-- sleeping on potatoes, and beets, hard surface. And his wife fed us once a day. In the evening, she came in with a big bucket with milk and some dough. They were called kluski-- dumplings, dumplings and milk. I was looking forward every day to the dumplings and milk. And I said, when I come out alive, I'll never want any other food, just lots of dumplings and milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you still eat dumplings and milk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still like dumplings-- no milk with it, but dumplings, Polish dumplings.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did you have any belongings with you that you had taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had this bag that my father gave me. And I was about 13 years old. And it was my talisman. I carried with me all through the entire war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you keep it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I-- in my hand. I never parted with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And so you're, you're in this house, and you're getting--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the only belonging. And I have a coat, and one pair of shoes, and one shirt. And that's how we lived for a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're in this house, and you're getting your dumplings and milk, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, in the, in the the basement.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the basement, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cellar, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband will tell you this. I don't remember the exact dates.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: And while we were in the cellar, we heard the shots every day. And this Ukrainian friend came in at night to inform us who was shot, who was caught, who was betrayed. These people were hiding. This was after was judenrein in the town. It was after the final solution. And our town was judenrein. And some people escaped from the ghetto. And they were hiding. And then they were caught. And they were shot. And we were-- couldn't talk, because we were afraid neighbors will hear. And then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he couldn't keep us any longer, because somebody overheard talking in the basement. And he told us to leave. There were five or six people. And then we went back to Stepaniuk's house during the night. We went back through fields. We went back to Stepaniuk's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a flashlight? How did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we just-- you just don't see anything. There was no lights. There were no street lights. It was dark, pitch dark. We just found the place-- instinct, survival. And then we got back to Stepaniuk's house. She let us in, my husband and me. And, and we stayed in one room. At this point, the Ukrainian police [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] joined the underground. They got the solution with the Germans. And a lot of them went into underground. And they were fighting the Germans and the Russians. So he was no longer there, Lusik. But she took care of us for many months. And she fed us. And she-- and we were just was an existence from minute to minute. We did know whether-- we didn't go out at all. We stayed in one room, hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't see daylight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very little-- we're just in one room, sitting, doing nothing. There's no reading, no watching, listen to radio or anything. We don't know what went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you-- you just sit there and do nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: All day?

speaker: interviewee
text: All day, just sit, nothing, just waiting for the meals, potatoes. Because she was poor at the time. She didn't have any food. We had mashed potatoes. She cooked three times a day mashed potatoes with bacon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you had never eaten bacon before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what did you think of it, eating it for the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was glad to have bacon. I would eat anything just to survive. Tasted good.

speaker: interviewer
text: It does. Did-- so you knew that it was bacon. And you knew it was not kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. yes. And still remember those mashed potatoes with bacon three times a day, because she didn't have anything else. And she was like a mother to us, very self-sacrificing. Should I tell you what happened to her?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from then, we went to-- she couldn't keep us anymore. And then we went to, to other forests. And we joined other people from our hometown. They were hiding until-- and we lived in the woods. Should I tell you life? We lived in the woods, on-- sleeping on the grass surrounded by many trees. They were all pine trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- you left her house. When did you leave to go into the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, my husband will give you the information.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And just, you just-- why did you leave? Why did you leave her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She couldn't keep us any longer, because some-- she was afraid of her neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And so you snuck out into the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- during the night, one friend-- who lives in Israel now-- he came and knocked on the window. Well, my husband had joined him before. I was left alone. And I jumped from the windows during the night. And then we walked through fields. And then we went into the woods. And then we met other people from our hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- in the woods, yes. And then we spent time in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you-- did you have a hut? Did you have-- what did you? You just slept under the stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slept under the stars, yes. And then was raining, you slept on the, on the wet grass.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in the woods for a short while near Ostrog. And then we all went into the woods on the Russian side, which they were occupied by the Germans at the time. And we were hiding there. It was not too far from was Ostrog. And we didn't have anything to eat. And we slept on the, on the grass. And we drank water from a pond where the horses used to make. And we lived there for a while. And during the night, we used to go out to steal food from the farmers. And we used to take out from their ground beets, onions, whatever. And then there was a very-- they were very good Gentile people. And they gave us some bread and some mil. And then we went back into the woods. Until one day, we were surrounded by, by the Nazis. They found out there were Jews hiding there. And we heard shooting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was separated from my husband. Everybody dispersed. We went into different directions. And I was running. And then I turned around. And I heard shots. And I turned around. And there was six Nazis with machine guns aiming at me. I had my Polish documents with me in my little pocket book. I took out the papers. And I raised my hands, both arms, as if to say, I'm not Jewish. Don't kill me. I'm just here in the woods looking for blueberries or whatever. And they continued shooting. And then they shot me in my right foot. I saw immediately lots of blood. The bullet went through the back, came-- and I saw the bullet coming out the front of the foot. [CRYING] And then I ran into the forest among the trees. And they lost me. And I went into this Ukrainian house. And she put me-- she couldn't take me into the house. She was afraid. Excuse me. She told me to go into the woods. She couldn't take me to the house. She saw my foot. She came out with bandages. She put some white, clean rags. And she left food for me. And she went back home. She was afraid. And I was laying there all night. I was unconscious. I developed high fever. I couldn't eat anything. And I just felt, crawling on my foot, big worms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were sucking the blood from my wounded food. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then towards morning, I heard my husband crying, calling my name. He was with some other people that survived. Some of them were shot. And then we went to-- this, this woman recommend a Russian doctor who was a partisan. He worked for the guerrillas at the time. There were Russian-- they formed Russian guerrillas in this area. And he was a doctor for the partisans, for the Russian, for the Russian guerrillas. And he knew who I was. And he bandaged the foot. And he said there was gangrene, to rush me to the hospital. He can't do anything. And then he arranged to get a, a, a wagon and horse. And my husband and I, we went to the hospital. And we resorted to our Polish documents. And we got to the hospital. We made up a story, that we were Polish. And the Ukrainians were burning, burning our homes. At that time, the Ukrainians were persecuting the Poles. And they wanted the Poles to get out. It was very chaotic at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was in the hospital. My husband got-- was working for the Germans, which he'll tell you separately. And the doctor in the hospital and the nurses, they decided they're going to amputate the whole leg. And then there was no penicillin. But they used some kind of antibiotic that they used for the soldiers, for the Russian soldiers. And then they were going to amputate. At that point, I didn't-- couldn't care less. I was so sick. I didn't know what was going on. And they didn't amputate the leg. They just amputated one thing, one toe. And the other toe is-- was hanging all the time. And I was in the hospital for many months under assumed name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only remember the first name, Stefania. I don't remember the last name. Nobody asked for the documents. The, the surgeon there, the orthopedic surgeon at the hospital, I imagine he never told me. But I-- he was Russian. And he knew who I was because he was also with the underground. He was also connected with the guerrillas. And he took care of me very well, never asked for my identity. He just knew. And I was placed in a ward in a tremendous room with about 10 people. And I developed very high fever. And then I started in my sleep, I remember speaking in Jewish. And they were all Gentile people in the room. And I said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I'm hot. And the fever was very high.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I woke up. And I sat down. And I said, oh, did I speak in German? And they said, yes, you said something in German. We didn't know you know German. And at that time, I was-- I fell asleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody moved away from me. Because the gangrene spread. And there was a terrible odor from the foot. So whoever was in bed next to me, they moved away. And I was in the hospital until we were liberated by the Russians. And then I walked on crutches. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- stop. I'm going to stop now. I need a rest. I need a break.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you just want to have some water? Or you want to stop the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just only for a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now what else? I missed a lot of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. Take your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you want to-- what kind of question do you want to ask me now?

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what, what did it feel like to get shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time-- oh, is this on tape now? No?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're on tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: What it felt?

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it is feel like?

speaker: interviewee
text: You're in a shock. You're in a daze. You don't feel anything. You're numb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel it? Is it hot going through you? I mean, I never--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, oh, how it feels to be shot? Yes, I felt that I was like a pinch, a very sharp pinch in the back of the foot. And then I saw the bullet come out through the front of the foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where the toe are. It was like a cross. And I, I don't remember feeling pain, just my instinct was to live, and to escape, and to run. I was a good runner at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I bet.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very athletic at the time. I was a-- I ran very fast. And they couldn't get me. They lost me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So you're, you're now out of the hospital. It's a month.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, out of the-- when we were liberated, we were out of the-- I was out of the hospital. And then through the Polish friend that my husband will tell you later, he arranged with a cousin that she didn't know our identity. She didn't know who we were. And we lived there for a while, just a couple of days. And then we went back to our home town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the false papers from?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband got it, because he had some connections from school. It was one of the teachers, I think, in high school, that made it for him for money.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And, and so you went back to Ostrog.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- there were no Jewish people. There was nobody. Everybody was looking at us. Where are we coming from? They never expected anybody to be alive. And I lived there with my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the town-- was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was completely bombarded, was, was a-- destroyed from the bombs. Like I told you before, it was six days war in our town. And it was awful. It was a horrendous experience to go back. And, and then they took my husband to the Russian army. He was mobilized to the Russian army after we came back. And I was left alone. And I went to live with the-- Mrs. Stepaniuk. She took me in. I lived with her. And this was a horrible experience. Because at that time, her son was caught and arrested by the Russians. They arrested many Ukrainians underground. And he was placed in prison. And I went with her, with the mother every day. She cooked potatoes with meat, whatever. And we went to that prison. She felt very secure with me. And I took care of her. And I promised myself for the rest of my life, I'll take her of her. She was like a mother to me. And I walked every day maybe two miles to deliver food. Later we found out they never gave him the food. All the prisoners were hung. They hung them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I remember, I went by my-- I went by myself to a Russian lieutenant or major. He was in the KGB. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I knocked on the door. And he asked me, what are you doing here? And I told him my whole history.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told him my past. I told him my husband is in the Russian army and what happened to my parents, what I lived through. And he was courteous to me. And he said, we can't do anything. And I came back three, four times. And he says, stop coming. Because they're not here anymore. And they were all killed. All the-- because he was caught. This Lusik was caught with the, with the, with arms. So that's why he was killed. And she found out. And of course, we cried together. And then a month or two later, during the night, it about 3 o'clock in the morning. We heard knocks on the door. And then we opened the door. It was a Russian soldier. And there was a wagon and a horse in front of the house. And they told her, we're giving you two hours to pack. You're going away. And I was standing there next to her. And I started to plead and cry. Please don't take her away. That's all I have left now. [CRYING] And I couldn't fight him at the time. He said, you just go inside. And she was placed on the, on the wagon. And they took her away. And I never saw her again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I knew they-- he told me. I says, where are you taking her? He told me quietly without her hearing, they're taking her. She's going to be shipped to Siberia, because she was the mother of a criminal. And they shipped her to Siberia. And I lost contact with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did contact her after I came to America. This was years later, because then they send everybody back to Ostrog, all the people that they were taking to Siberia, they sent her back to Ostrog. And I wrote a letter to the mayor of Ostrog. I wrote a letter to other people. I found her address. She didn't live there any longer at the same address. And I contacted her. And I sent her packages. And then she told me, please stop sending packages because there is an awful lot of money she has to pay in duty. And it doesn't pay. And later, I found out that she died in Ostrog. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I lived alone after she was taken to Siberia. I lived alone just one day. And I had very dear friends that they knew my parents. This woman was with a child, also survivors. We were together in the woods all the time. And her husband was the one that had escaped to Russia during the-- remember, I told you that how the Russians chased people out like my sister-- with yeah, my sister and my brother, that they escaped to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this, my friend's husband, escaped to Russia. He contacted his wife. Dora Siroka was her name, with a child, Rita. And my-- she told him that I am one of the very few survivors. There are only 40 survivors. And I'm one of them and how we went-- we lived in the woods together. And he told her, take her into your house. Whatever money I make, I'll send for both of you. And make sure that she has enough of everything. Share everything with her. And I-- and she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we lived in Ostrog in one room, in one little kitchen, the three of us. My husband was in the Russian army already. And I remember I had to-- I wanted to reciprocate and do something. I used to get up very early in the morning and try to get water from a water-- what do you call it? Well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well. The well was always frozen on the outside. And I stood there with a big bucket, with hand-- by hand, I turned the, the, the handle until the bucket went down to the well. And then the big bucket with the water came up. And I carried the water to the house. That's the only water we had. It's from the well. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we lived there for a while, the three of us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then there was some kind of agreement that Polish citizens can go to the west, to Lodz or other cities. So I went with this Dora Siroka and her daughter, Rita. We went to Lodz. And I lived with them again. And then her husband came back from Russia and lived with them for a while. And then I was able to get a job through the Polish friends, the one that helped us during the war. I got a job as an interpreter in-- was like an army unit. They were interrogating. They were arresting Polish underground at the time. And I was an interpreter.
